why_jesus_said_it: In Matthew 25, Jesus instructs us that feeding the hungry and burying the dead are criteria for eternal life. Tobias' acts of charity in exile directly foreshadow Christ's call to corporate works of mercy.
ccc_validation: CCC 2447: The works of mercy are charitable actions by which we come to the aid of our neighbor. Burying the dead is a corporal work of mercy.
historical_reality: The deportation of Israel under Assyrian king Shalmaneser V in 722 BC forced captives to choose between total cultural assimilation and keeping their faith through dangerous acts of charity.
translation_shift: The Latin Vulgate translation of Tobias highlights 'almsdeeds' (elemosynas) which represents the Greek 'eleemosyne', translating the Hebrew 'tzedakah' (righteousness/charity).
fascinating_fact: The Book of Tobias was historically kept in the canon by the early Church fathers (e.g. Augustine) specifically for its practical guides on living out charity in hostile environments.
